Collection agency refusing to provide debt validation? This violates federal law. Learn your FDCPA rights.

Collection Agency Won't Verify Debt: Refusing Validation Under FDCPA

Understanding Your Situation

A collection agency is demanding payment for a medical debt, but when you requested validation (proof that you owe the debt), they ignored your request, provided insufficient documentation, or outright refused to validate the debt. This violates federal law. Under the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA, 15 U.S.C. § 1692g), debt collectors are legally required to provide verification of the debt upon your written request within 30 days of their initial contact. If they fail to validate the debt, they must cease collection activity and cannot report the debt to credit bureaus. You have powerful legal protections when collectors violate validation requirements.

Why This Happens

Lack of Documentation: The collection agency may not have sufficient documentation from the original healthcare provider to verify the debt—so they stall or refuse validation requests.

Purchased Debt with Incomplete Records: Medical debt is often sold to collection agencies for pennies on the dollar, sometimes without complete medical records or billing documentation.

Hoping You'll Pay Without Verification: Many consumers don't know their validation rights. Collectors may ignore requests, hoping you'll pay out of fear or confusion.

Systematic FDCPA Violations: Some collection agencies routinely violate validation requirements, especially smaller or unscrupulous agencies.

Multiple Debt Buyers: When debt is sold multiple times, documentation often gets lost or incomplete through each transfer.

Time Delays and Stalling Tactics: Collectors may delay sending validation hoping you'll forget about the request or the statute of limitations will run out.

Insufficient Validation Provided: Collectors may send a generic letter or computer printout that doesn't actually verify the debt's legitimacy, amount, or your responsibility.

What To Check

✓ Verify you sent validation request within 30 days: Did you request validation in writing within 30 days of the collector's first contact?
✓ Confirm you sent via certified mail: Do you have proof of delivery (certified mail receipt)?
✓ Review what you requested: Did you clearly request validation including: proof of debt amount, original creditor, proof you owe the debt?
✓ Check what collector sent (if anything): Did they provide actual documentation or just a generic statement?
✓ Document all violations: Note dates of contact, refused validation, continued collection activity, credit reporting
✓ Review your credit reports: Is the debt still being reported despite lack of validation?
✓ Check collector's continued actions: Are they still calling, sending letters, or threatening legal action despite not validating?
✓ Verify collector is licensed: Check if the collection agency is licensed in your state (many states require licensing)

What To Do Next

Step 1: Send Second Demand for Validation
Send another written validation request via certified mail specifically citing FDCPA violations:

'I previously requested validation of the alleged debt [account number] on [date] via certified mail. You failed to provide validation as required by 15 U.S.C. § 1692g(b). Under the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act, you are required to cease all collection activity until you have provided verification of this debt. Despite your failure to validate, you have continued collection activity [specify: phone calls, letters, credit reporting]. I demand immediate cessation of all collection activity, removal of any credit reporting, and provision of complete debt validation documentation including: itemized statement from original creditor, proof of your authority to collect this debt, and documentation proving I owe this debt. Continued collection activity without validation constitutes willful FDCPA violations and will result in legal action.'

Use our Medical Collections Debt Validation tool to generate a formal validation demand letter.

Step 2: Document All FDCPA Violations
Create a detailed log:
• Date and method of initial contact from collector
• Date you sent validation request
• Proof of delivery (certified mail tracking)
• All subsequent collection attempts (calls, letters, credit reports)
• Collector's failure to cease activity
• Any harassment, threats, or false statements

These violations may entitle you to statutory damages up to $1,000 plus actual damages and attorney fees.

Step 3: Dispute with Credit Bureaus
If the debt is on your credit reports without validation, dispute it with all three bureaus:

'I am disputing this collection account. I requested debt validation from the collector under FDCPA on [date], and they failed to provide verification as required by law. Under the Fair Credit Reporting Act, unverified debts should not be reported. I demand immediate removal of this account from my credit report.'

Use our Medical Credit Report Removal tool to generate dispute letters for credit bureaus.

Step 4: File Complaints with Regulators
File official complaints with:
• Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB): Submit CFPB Complaint
• Federal Trade Commission (FTC): Report to FTC
• Your State Attorney General: Consumer protection division
• Your State Department of Financial Regulation: If state requires debt collector licensing

Include all documentation of validation requests and violations in your complaints.

Step 5: Send Cease and Desist Letter (Optional)
If harassment continues, send a cease and desist letter demanding they stop all contact except to notify you of specific legal actions. Note: This stops contact but doesn't make the debt go away—they can still sue you.

Step 6: Consult Consumer Rights Attorney
If the collector continues violating FDCPA after your complaints:
• Consult a consumer rights attorney specializing in FDCPA cases
• Many take cases on contingency (you don't pay unless you win)
• FDCPA allows you to recover attorney fees, statutory damages ($1,000), and actual damages
• Documented violations can result in settlements or judgments in your favor

Step 7: Consider FDCPA Lawsuit
You have one year from the date of violation to file an FDCPA lawsuit. Violations include:
• Failing to provide validation after written request
• Continuing collection activity during validation period
• Reporting to credit bureaus without validation
• Making false statements or threats
• Harassment or abusive conduct

For more guidance on medical debt and collections, see our Medical Debt Hub.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens if a collection agency never validates the debt?

If a collector fails to validate the debt after your written request, they are legally required to cease collection activity under FDCPA § 1692g(b). They cannot continue calling, cannot sue you for the debt, and cannot report it to credit bureaus. If they do any of these, they're violating federal law and you can sue them for damages. Additionally, credit bureaus must remove unverified collection accounts when properly disputed.

How long do collectors have to provide validation?

There's no specific deadline in the FDCPA, but courts generally find that collectors must provide validation within a reasonable time (typically 30 days or less from your request). More importantly, collectors MUST cease all collection activity immediately upon receiving your validation request until they provide verification. Continuing to collect without validation is a clear violation.

What counts as sufficient debt validation?

Sufficient validation should include: (1) itemized statement from the original creditor showing services/dates/charges, (2) proof that you are responsible for the debt, (3) proof that the collector has legal authority to collect the debt (chain of ownership if debt was sold), and (4) breakdown of current balance including any added fees. A generic computer printout or simple statement of the amount owed is NOT sufficient validation.

Can I sue a collection agency for not validating?

Yes. FDCPA violations allow you to sue for actual damages (harm caused), statutory damages up to $1,000, and attorney fees. If you have documented that you requested validation and the collector refused to provide it, continued collecting, or made false statements, you have grounds for a lawsuit. Consult a consumer rights attorney—many offer free consultations and work on contingency.

What if the debt is legitimate but the collector won't validate?

Even if you know you owe a medical debt, collectors are still legally required to validate it upon request. If they can't or won't provide validation, they can't legally collect. You can use lack of validation to negotiate debt removal or settlement. Once they validate with proper documentation, then you can assess whether to negotiate payment, dispute the amount, or request financial assistance from the original healthcare provider.

Legal Disclaimer

Educational purposes only. This information is not legal, medical, or financial advice. FDCPA rights and debt collection laws are complex. Consult a consumer rights attorney for personalized legal guidance.
